column5: Developer of advanced optical sensing solutions that provide superior accuracy when measuring voltage, current, and temperature
column6: Micatu Inc. operates as a developer of optical sensing solutions tailored for the power and industrial sectors. Founded in 2011 by Michael Oshetski, the company is headquartered in Horseheads, New York, with a mission to fundamentally change how the world measures with light. Oshetski, a graduate of the Rochester Institute of Technology with a B.S. in Electrical Engineering, brings over two decades of experience in the optics industry to his role as CEO. His background in managing product development and his expertise in optics have been central to the company's direction. The company's core business revolves around providing highly accurate measurement and analytics for electrical grids through its modular, optical sensing technology platform. Micatu serves a clientele primarily composed of electric utility companies and industrial businesses, offering them tools to modernize their grids. Revenue is generated through the sale of its hardware and accompanying software platforms. The business model focuses on delivering solutions that are safer, more accurate, and more affordable than legacy systems, which is particularly relevant as grids become more complex with the integration of renewable energy sources. Micatu's flagship product is the GridView optical sensing platform, which measures voltage, current, temperature, and vibration with high fidelity. This platform utilizes light passed through an optical crystal to measure the electric field, a non-conductive method that enhances safety by avoiding the risks of saturation and combustion associated with traditional sensors. The sensors can be deployed on overhead lines, underground, and even in groundless configurations, and are designed to retrofit into existing grid infrastructures. A key feature is their high sample rate of 15,000 samples per cycle, providing granular data that is crucial for managing grid disruptions and integrating distributed energy resources. The platform includes the certusEDGE Edge Processing Unit, which can measure up to the 2500th harmonic, offering deep power quality analytics and enabling autonomous local responses to grid disturbances. This technology provides utilities with actionable data to improve power quality, manage safety, and enhance overall grid resilience. The company's journey began in a garage in 2011 and has seen significant milestones, including winning the $1 million grand prize in the 76West Clean Energy Business Competition. Micatu has raised a total of $10 million over three funding rounds, with a notable Series A round of $10 million in January 2021, led by WAVE Equity Partners. This funding supports the company's global expansion and the scaled manufacturing of its GridView platform to meet growing demand. Keywords: optical sensing, grid modernization, power quality monitoring, smart grid sensors, utility technology, real-time grid data, voltage and current measurement, renewable energy integration, grid resilience, edge computing for utilities, harmonic distortion measurement, non-conductive sensors, electric grid analytics, underground sensors, overhead line monitoring, industrial sensors, distributed energy resources (DER), optical transformers, energy infrastructure, electrical grid safety, substation automation, power system measurement, data-driven grid management, advanced metering infrastructure, smart grid solutions

column5: Alcohol marketplace to shop beer, wine and spirits
column6: Drizly was a startup that specialized in the delivery of alcoholic beverages. The company operated in the e-commerce and on-demand delivery market, providing a convenient way for customers to order alcohol online and have it delivered to their doorstep. Drizly primarily served individual consumers who wanted to purchase beer, wine, and spirits without having to visit a physical store.The business model of Drizly was based on partnering with local liquor stores. When a customer placed an order through the Drizly app or website, the order was routed to a nearby partner store, which then handled the delivery. Drizly made money by charging these partner stores a fee for using its platform and by adding a delivery fee to the customer's order. This model allowed Drizly to offer a wide selection of products without holding any inventory itself.Drizly operated in a competitive market that included other on-demand delivery services and traditional brick-and-mortar liquor stores. The company's value proposition was the convenience and speed of delivery, often promising delivery within an hour. This appealed to busy consumers and those looking for last-minute purchases.However, Drizly is no longer in service. Customers looking for similar services are now directed to Uber Eats, which has taken over the alcohol delivery needs that Drizly once fulfilled.Keywords: alcohol delivery, e-commerce, on-demand service, local liquor stores, consumer convenience, delivery fee, partner stores, competitive market, Drizly, Uber Eats.

column5: Creates bespoke mini-proteins to help drug hunters deliver life-saving treatments
column6: Ordaos Bio is a pioneering startup that leverages generative AI to revolutionize the drug discovery process. Traditional methods of drug discovery are often slow, costly, and have a high failure rate. Ordaos Bio aims to address these challenges by using advanced artificial intelligence to accelerate the development of life-saving medicines. Generative AI, unlike predictive AI, can create new data and solutions, making it particularly effective in identifying potential drug candidates that might be overlooked by conventional methods.The company primarily serves biopharmaceutical companies that are looking to streamline their drug development pipelines. By partnering with these firms, Ordaos Bio helps reduce the time and cost associated with bringing new drugs to market. This is crucial in the fight against diseases where current capabilities fall short due to financial and temporal constraints.Operating in the healthcare and biotechnology market, Ordaos Bio's business model revolves around offering AI-driven drug discovery services. They generate revenue through partnerships and collaborations with biopharmaceutical companies, who pay for access to Ordaos Bio's cutting-edge technology and expertise. This model not only provides a steady income stream but also positions Ordaos Bio as a key player in the future of medical research.The team at Ordaos Bio is composed of visionaries in AI, digital drug discovery, academic research, and drug development. They are committed to creating a paradigm shift in the industry, making the hidden aspects of drug discovery visible and actionable through their innovative use of generative AI.In summary, Ordaos Bio is transforming the landscape of drug discovery by making it faster, cheaper, and more efficient through the use of generative AI. Their focus on collaboration with biopharmaceutical companies ensures that they are at the forefront of bringing new, life-saving drugs to market.Keywords: Generative AI, Drug Discovery, Biopharmaceutical, Healthcare, Biotechnology, Innovation, Cost Reduction, Efficiency, Medical Research, Partnerships.

column5: Mobile outreach and case management for homelessness
column6: The Rehumanization Project is a non-profit organization dedicated to assisting individuals affected by homelessness. The organization's efforts are centered around providing a pathway to stability and self-sufficiency for this vulnerable population. It was founded in March 2022. The core of its operations involves direct, on-the-ground support through several key programs.One of its primary services is mobile outreach, where teams actively engage with homeless individuals in their environments to build trust and offer immediate assistance. This is complemented by comprehensive case management services, which provide continuous and personalized support tailored to each individual's needs. These services aim to address the root causes of homelessness and help clients navigate the complexities of accessing housing, healthcare, and employment resources.The organization collaborates with a network of communities, partners, and individual volunteers to deliver its services. This collaborative model allows it to leverage a broader range of resources and expertise, enhancing its ability to effect change. By working with service providers, donors, and advocates, The Rehumanization Project aims to build a community-wide response to homelessness, with the ultimate goal of making it a rare, brief, and non-recurring experience.Keywords: homelessness assistance, mobile outreach, case management, community collaboration, homeless support, non-profit, social services, self-sufficiency programs, homeless advocacy, community outreach, stability services, housing support, street outreach, vulnerable populations support, social impact

column5: Dedicated to teach girls aged 7 to 17 about computer programming and digital technology
column6: Black Girls CODE is a pioneering startup dedicated to empowering young Black girls by providing them with the skills and opportunities to excel in the technology sector. The company operates primarily in the education and technology markets, focusing on STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics) education. Their primary clients are young Black girls, typically aged 7-17, who are interested in learning about coding, app development, robotics, and other tech-related fields.The business model of Black Girls CODE revolves around offering workshops, summer camps, and virtual classes that teach various aspects of technology and programming. These educational programs are designed to be engaging and accessible, ensuring that participants not only learn valuable skills but also enjoy the process. The company generates revenue through tuition fees for these programs. Additionally, they offer financial need-based scholarships to ensure that economic barriers do not prevent interested students from participating.Black Girls CODE also partners with corporations and educational institutions to expand its reach and impact. These partnerships often involve sponsorships, grants, and collaborative events, which provide additional revenue streams and resources for the organization. By fostering a supportive community and creating a pipeline of talented young Black women in tech, Black Girls CODE aims to address the underrepresentation of Black women in the technology industry.In summary, Black Girls CODE is a mission-driven organization that combines education and technology to create opportunities for young Black girls. They operate in the STEM education market, serving a niche but crucial demographic. Their business model includes tuition-based programs, scholarships, and corporate partnerships, all aimed at making tech education accessible and enjoyable.Keywords: STEM education, coding workshops, Black girls, technology, virtual camps, app development, robotics, scholarships, partnerships, empowerment.

column6: Light Field Lab is a cutting-edge technology startup that operates in the digital display market. The company is revolutionizing the way we perceive reality by creating a world where digital content escapes the screen and merges seamlessly with the physical world. This concept, once thought to be limited to science fiction, is now a reality thanks to Light Field Lab's innovative technology.The company's proprietary technology includes high-performance FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Array, a type of customizable digital circuit), electronics, wall and display controllers, and a photonics array. They also utilize a nanoparticle polymer fused surface energy relay, which is a complex way of saying they use tiny particles to manipulate light and create their holographic displays. Light Field Lab's business model revolves around selling their advanced display technology to clients who want to create immersive, holographic experiences. These clients could range from theme parks looking to create next-level attractions, to businesses wanting to hold more engaging presentations, to entertainment companies aiming to create more realistic virtual reality experiences. The company generates revenue by selling their display systems and licensing their technology. They've already made significant strides in their field, with their SolidLight holographic display and Defy™ experiences being recognized as some of the top inventions of 2022.In summary, Light Field Lab is a pioneering startup that's pushing the boundaries of what's possible in the digital display market. Their innovative technology is set to disrupt traditional flat images and redefine our perception of reality.Keywords: Light Field Lab, Digital Display, Holographic Technology, FPGA, Photonics Array, Nanoparticle Polymer, SolidLight, Defy™ Experiences, Virtual Reality, Immersive Experiences.

column5: Provides solutions for mobility, smart content management, and video security systems
column6: Edgetensor Technologies, a Dallas-based company founded in 2017 by Rajesh Narasimha and Soumitry Ray, operates at the intersection of artificial intelligence and mobility. The firm is focused on developing 'AI at the Edge' technology, which involves processing data locally on devices rather than sending it to the cloud. This approach is designed to overcome challenges related to speed, latency, privacy, and cost associated with cloud computing. Both founders hold Ph.D.s from the Georgia Institute of Technology and previously worked at Metaio, an augmented reality startup that was acquired by Apple in 2015. Their collective background includes extensive experience in computer vision, machine learning, and imaging algorithms, supported by over 75 shared patents and publications. The company's core offering is an AI-driven analytics platform for the automotive and fleet management industry. Edgetensor provides a software development kit (SDK) and a comprehensive fleet management dashboard that can be integrated with a wide range of cameras and low-cost computing hardware. The technology is capable of real-time monitoring and analysis of driver behavior, tracking facial analytics like gaze direction, head pose, and emotion to detect and alert for distracted driving. Its proprietary AI inference engine is optimized to run efficiently on devices with limited processing power. In July 2023, the company launched Edgetensor 2.0, an upgraded version of its software featuring a Liability Risk Scorecard, predictive analytics, and improved data accuracy to provide more actionable insights. Edgetensor's business model is B2B, generating revenue by selling its solutions to telematics service providers, fleet system integrators, automotive TIER1 suppliers, and OEMs. The company's solutions aim to enhance driver safety, improve operational efficiency by reducing false alarms, and provide a holistic view of business KPIs. Beyond fleet management, the technology has potential applications in smart cities for automating parking, monitoring access to areas, and in digital advertising. The company has secured funding through several rounds, including investments from SRI Capital, 3Lines Venture Capital, and Walden International, to support its growth and market expansion. Keywords: fleet management, AI at the Edge, driver monitoring system, telematics, computer vision, automotive safety, edge computing, AI analytics platform, ADAS, video telematics, driver safety, operational efficiency, distracted driving detection, facial analytics, mobility tech, smart city solutions, AI SDK, vehicle safety technology, data-driven fleet management, insurtech

column5: Wholesale renewable electricity procurement and management platform
column6: Power Synch is a wholesale electric platform created by American PowerNet, designed for buyers to request power bundled with Renewable Energy Credits (RECs) and for sellers to bid on those requests with guaranteed payment upon delivery. The platform addresses the challenge of connecting large electricity buyers and sellers in a market that is increasingly fragmented with smaller, sustainable generation farms. It allows buyers to competitively request wholesale blocks of sustainable power, with a minimum size of 1 MW for one month, making the wholesale renewable market accessible to a wider range of organizations that cannot commit to long-term power purchase agreements. Power Synch's parent company, American PowerNet, was founded in 1994 by R. Scott Helm. Helm has over twenty-five years of experience in the energy industry, having previously founded the energy auditing firm Expense Audit & Consulting Co. He developed American PowerNet to provide large-scale consumers with direct access to wholesale electric markets. Over the years, American PowerNet has been a pioneer in wholesale electricity procurement, introducing innovative models that have been adopted by Fortune 500 companies, universities, and manufacturers. In December 2025, American PowerNet was acquired by Axpo U.S. LLC, a deal that expanded Axpo's presence in the US retail energy market. Following the acquisition, American PowerNet became a wholly owned subsidiary of Axpo US, with Scott Helm and his team continuing to deliver services under Power Synch LLC. The business model centers on facilitating transactions in the wholesale renewable electricity market. Power Synch leverages American PowerNet's established infrastructure of ISO memberships and state supplier licenses to manage the complexities of physical power delivery from the wholesale market to retail meters. The platform serves large commercial, industrial, government, and university entities. Revenue is generated through a transparent, flat-rate management fee structure, a contrast to competitors who often charge based on usage. column5: Marketing and advertising company specializing in digital marketing and branding services
column6: Partners + Napier is a full-service creative and media advertising agency headquartered in Rochester, New York, with additional offices in New York City and Boston. The firm was established in 2004 through a management buyout led by Sharon Napier, who now serves as Executive Chair, alongside three partners: Jeff Gabel, and Jim DiNoto. They purchased the Rochester and Atlanta offices of their former employer, Wolf Group Integrated Communications, after its parent company decided to shut down the firm. This founding group of executives, along with 40 initial employees, formed the new agency with a core philosophy that business is personal. Sharon Napier's entrepreneurial drive was inspired by her immigrant parents. After starting her career with a degree in social work, she discovered a passion for marketing while working on a political campaign. This led her to the advertising industry, where she became president of two offices at the Wolf Group before deciding to pursue her master's degree at age 43 and subsequently launch her own agency. Courtney Cotrupe, one of the original employees from 2004, was promoted to CEO in 2019, while Napier transitioned to her current role focusing on strategic initiatives and client relationships. The agency operates on a service-based business model, providing integrated creative, media, production, and design capabilities for its clients. It specializes in several key markets, including health and wellness, consumer packaged goods (CPG) food and beverage, restaurants, beverage alcohol, and B2B enterprise services. The company's revenue is generated by creating and executing advertising campaigns and providing strategic marketing services for a diverse client roster that includes names like Constellation Brands, Highmark Health, Smashburger, and Xerox. A significant milestone occurred in 2011 when Partners + Napier was acquired by Project Worldwide, an independent, employee-owned global network of agencies. This acquisition provided the agency with a global footprint while allowing it to maintain its distinct culture. The firm has received numerous accolades for its campaign effectiveness, including being ranked by Effie Worldwide as one of the Most Effective Agencies in North America. column5: Serverless data warehouse leveraging DuckDB for analytics
column6: MotherDuck is a serverless data warehouse built on the open-source DuckDB in-process analytics database. Founded in 2022 and headquartered in Seattle, Washington, the company aims to simplify data analytics by combining the speed of DuckDB with the scalability and collaborative features of the cloud. MotherDuck targets use cases for both small and large datasets, challenging the idea that all data problems are "big data" problems. The company was founded by Jordan Tigani, Tino Tereshko, Ryan Boyd, and Leila Horejsi. The founding team possesses extensive experience from innovative data companies like Google BigQuery, SingleStore, and Firebolt. Tigani, the CEO, was a founding engineer and product leader for Google BigQuery and saw an opportunity to create a serverless version of the fast-growing DuckDB. He partnered with DuckDB's creators, Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt of DuckDB Labs, who became co-founders of the new venture. Tino Tereshko, another BigQuery veteran, joined as a co-founder and serves as VP of Product. The name "MotherDuck" was suggested by Lloyd Tabb, the founder of Looker. MotherDuck has raised a total of $100 million in funding over three rounds, reaching a valuation of $400 million by September 2023. Its investors include Andreessen Horowitz, Redpoint Ventures, Felicis, and Madrona Venture Group. The product officially launched in June 2023. The platform operates on a unique hybrid execution model, allowing queries to be processed locally on a user's machine, in the cloud, or both, optimizing for speed and cost-efficiency. This architecture, which the company calls "hypertenancy," provides each user with their own isolated compute instance (a "duckling") to prevent resource contention and ensure consistent performance. The system is designed for a variety of users, including data analysts, engineers, and data scientists, and integrates with popular tools in the modern data stack like dbt, Fivetran, and Tableau. MotherDuck's platform adds essential features for organizational use that are not native to DuckDB, such as cloud storage, sharing, collaboration, and security. Its business model is expected to be a mix of subscription and usage-based pricing, aiming for a low cost of entry. The platform also incorporates AI features, like a SQL fixer and the ability to generate SQL queries from natural language. Keywords: serverless data warehouse, DuckDB, cloud analytics, hybrid execution, OLAP, business intelligence, SQL analytics, data processing, hypertenancy, data collaboration, embedded analytics, lakehouse, customer-facing analytics, data pipelines, AI-powered data workflows

column5: A scale-out, cloud-based storage and data management solution
column6: Founded in 2010, Exablox developed scale-out network-attached storage (NAS) solutions targeting the runaway storage costs and information management challenges faced by organizations. In January 2017, the company was acquired by StorageCraft Technology Corporation, a move designed to merge primary and secondary storage solutions. The acquisition integrated Exablox's hardware expertise with StorageCraft's data protection software portfolio. Douglas Brockett, who was the CEO of Exablox, became the president of StorageCraft following the transaction. Exablox's core business centered on providing a cloud-managed, object-based storage appliance called OneBlox. This product was designed for both primary and secondary storage, serving a client base that included Fortune 500 companies, research institutions, and government entities. The company operated on a model that allowed customers to purchase the appliance and use their own commodity hard drives, significantly lowering the total cost of ownership by avoiding the markup typical of legacy storage vendors. The flagship product, OneBlox, is a scale-out storage appliance built on an object-based file system. Its architecture enables seamless expansion by adding more drives or appliances without downtime or complex reconfiguration, presenting a single global file system. Key features include inline variable-length deduplication, compression, continuous data protection through unlimited snapshots, and multi-site replication for disaster recovery. The system was designed for ease of use, managed through a cloud-based service called OneSystem, which allowed for remote monitoring and management from any browser. OneBlox abandoned traditional RAID concepts in favor of replicating and distributing data objects across multiple drives and nodes to protect against failures. This design supported both SMB and NFS protocols, making it suitable for virtualized environments like VMware and Hyper-V. Keywords: scale-out storage, object-based storage, network-attached storage, data protection, storage appliance, deduplication, cloud-managed storage, OneBlox, enterprise storage, data management

column5: Strax Intelligence Group | Smarter Response Solutions
column6: Strax Intelligence Group provides a sophisticated common operating platform engineered for real-time incident response, serving a client base that includes public safety agencies, government entities, and private security organizations. The company was founded in 2015 by Scott Adams, a serial entrepreneur with a track record of building and scaling technology businesses. Adams previously co-founded Hiway Technologies, a major web hosting company, and served as Chairman of MobileHelp, which created the first mobile personal emergency response system. This background in scalable tech and emergency systems provided a direct pathway to establishing Strax.The firm's core offering is the STRAX Platform, a cloud-native solution that addresses the challenge of siloed information systems in crisis management. It achieves this by aggregating disparate data sources—including priority alerts, sensor data, and video feeds from public, private, manned, and unmanned systems like drones—into a single, map-centric operational view. This unification of data allows for enhanced situational awareness, enabling command structures to make faster, more coordinated decisions. The platform's open architecture is designed for extensive data fusion, integrating everything from legacy systems to modern IoT devices and AI-powered alerts. Functionally, it can detect a threat, such as a weapon appearing on a camera feed, and instantly notify the appropriate authorities. The business generates revenue by providing its platform and services to its clients, operating in the public safety and security technology market. A significant milestone for the company occurred in March 2024, when it was acquired by Greater Sum Ventures (GSV), an entrepreneurial family office, to become part of a larger suite of public safety technology solutions alongside companies like Utility Associates and SOMA Global. This acquisition is intended to fund further innovation in data aggregation and streamline emergency response capabilities. Following the acquisition, Scott Adams continues to lead the company as CEO. In April 2024, Strax announced a strategic partnership with Utility Associates to link its real-time crime center platform with Utility's digital evidence management system, creating a more unified ecosystem for law enforcement. column5: Pakt | Minimalist travel gear for conscious travelers
column6: Pakt operates as a direct-to-consumer internet brand specializing in travel gear for environmentally-conscious and design-savvy travelers. The company's genesis is a story of serendipity; it was officially founded in 2017 by industrial designer Malcolm Fontier after a discontinued bag he had designed years prior, the Getaway, was featured in the Netflix documentary "Minimalism". This feature sparked thousands of requests for the bag's return, leading to a highly successful Indiegogo crowdfunding campaign that raised over $2 million and sold nearly 10,000 units of the reimagined "Pakt One" duffel bag. Founder Malcolm Fontier has a background in industrial design and a history of entrepreneurship, including a designer lifestyle accessories line and an online community for motorcyclists. His passion for travel and product design, combined with an environmental consciousness, forms the foundation of the company's ethos. Concurrent with Pakt, Fontier launched SeaHive, an initiative focused on reducing plastic waste in packaging, a principle applied to all Pakt products which are shipped in 100% plastic-free packaging. Pakt's business model is centered on e-commerce, selling directly to consumers through its website, a strategy that originated from its crowdfunding success. The product line, developed with significant input from its customer community, includes backpacks, duffel bags, slings, and various travel accessories. Key products like the Pakt Travel Backpack, created in collaboration with gear expert Chase Reeves, feature a clamshell, dual-compartment design that offers the organization of a suitcase with the convenience of a backpack. Features are tailored for modern travel, including padded laptop compartments, dedicated TSA pockets for easier security checks, and construction from durable, water-resistant, and recycled materials like rPET. The company emphasizes creating versatile, long-lasting products to encourage consumers to buy fewer items, and it backs this with a lifetime guarantee and a repair and resale program to keep products out of landfills. column5: Provides marketing technology, artificial intelligence, and self-learning
column6: OfferFit.ai is a startup that operates in the marketing technology sector, providing an innovative solution to the traditional method of A/B testing. The company uses artificial intelligence (AI) to automate and individualize marketing decisions, replacing the slow and impersonal manual testing process. OfferFit's AI testing allows businesses to use their first-party data to determine the optimal message, product, incentive, channel, and timing for each customer. This approach is more efficient and effective, enabling businesses to test everything simultaneously and adapt to market changes instantly.OfferFit's clients are primarily businesses that require a more efficient and personalized approach to marketing. These include companies in various industries, such as sports and entertainment, as evidenced by testimonials from Kayo Sports & BINGE. The company's business model revolves around providing its AI testing service to these businesses, helping them increase their conversion rates and customer lifetime value. OfferFit generates revenue by charging these businesses for its services.The company's solution has proven to be highly effective, with clients reporting significant increases in conversion value. For instance, Kayo Sports & BINGE reported a 30% increase in additional conversion value after using OfferFit's services. This indicates that OfferFit's AI testing can provide substantial financial benefits to businesses, making it a promising startup in the marketing technology sector.Keywords: AI Testing, Marketing Technology, Personalized Marketing, Conversion Rate, Customer Lifetime Value, First-Party Data, Automated Decision-Making, Market Adaptation, Efficiency, Innovation.

column5: AI-powered platform converting Figma designs into applications
column6: App2.dev is an AI-powered, no-code/low-code development platform that enables users to build web and mobile applications from ideas or Figma designs. The company was founded in July 2025 by Nick Gatzoulis, a serial entrepreneur and technologist who serves as the Founder and CEO. Gatzoulis's background as a tech lead and his experience in scaling tech companies and product development led him to create a tool aimed at democratizing software development. The platform's core function is to transform natural language descriptions and Figma designs into production-ready code for web and mobile apps. It achieves this by leveraging a suite of AI models, including those from OpenAI, Google Gemini, and Anthropic (Claude), to generate the code. This process includes creating both the frontend and backend, complete with a database and user authentication. App2.dev is designed for a wide range of users, from non-technical founders and designers to experienced developers seeking to accelerate their workflow. Key features include real-time development in a secure sandbox environment, one-click deployment through integrations with GitHub and Vercel, and collaborative workspaces for teams. The platform supports React Native for mobile app development. Currently in an early access phase, App2.dev offers a free trial, with pricing information available on its website. The company's vision extends to becoming a 'Startup-as-a-Service' launchpad, facilitating not just building and deploying but also funding and growth for new applications. Keywords: AI-powered development, no-code, low-code, Figma to code, application development, web app builder, mobile app builder, software development, generative AI, production-ready code, startup-as-a-service, developer tools, UI/UX, frontend development, backend development, database authentication, real-time development, code generation, React Native, natural language processing

column5: Filtration and treatment systems for ships
column6: Ecochlor, Inc. provides ballast water management systems (BWMSs) for the global shipping industry, targeting clients such as tanker and bulk carrier owners with mid-to-high ballast water flow rates. The company generates revenue through the sale, installation, and servicing of its systems, as well as the recurring supply of the treatment chemicals required for operation. Founded in 2001 by Tom Perlich, who brought decades of experience from the chemical and industrial sectors, Ecochlor was established to commercialize patented ballast water treatment technologies. Perlich's background includes engineering and sales roles at Nalco Water and ExxonMobil, providing a strong foundation for developing solutions for the maritime industry.The company's core technology utilizes chlorine dioxide (ClO2), a biocide generated on board the vessel, to disinfect ballast water during uptake. This method is designed to be effective across all water conditions, including variations in salinity, temperature, and turbidity, without requiring neutralization before discharge. This core technology is delivered through a product line offering flexibility to shipowners. The offerings include the filter-based Ecochlor® BWMS, the filterless EcoOne® BWMS for marine and brackish waters, and the EcoOne® Hybrid, which allows operators to switch between filtered and filterless modes. These systems feature low power consumption and modular designs to accommodate space limitations on vessels.Ecochlor marked a significant milestone in 2004 with its first system installation on the 'Atlantic Compass'. A subsequent installation on the 'Moku Pahu' in 2006 operated for 13 years under strict California regulations, demonstrating the system's durability. The company achieved IMO Type Approval in 2011 and US Coast Guard Type Approval in 2017, critical regulatory validations for market access. Recognized for its rapid expansion, Ecochlor was listed on the Inc. 500 in 2017 with a three-year revenue growth of 972%. In November 2024, Ecochlor was acquired by ERMA FIRST, a Greece-based competitor, a move intended to consolidate their position in the global BWMS market.Keywords: ballast water management system, BWMS, chlorine dioxide water treatment, marine environmental protection, ship ballast water, invasive species prevention, maritime water treatment, Ecochlor, EcoOne, ship retrofit solutions, marine compliance, USCG Type Approval, IMO certified, low power BWMS, filterless ballast water system, chemical disinfection BWTS, marine engineering, vessel water safety, green marine technology, tanker ballast systems, bulk carrier water treatment

column5: Integrated photonic sensors for microphysiological systems, biomanufacturing, and drug discovery
column6: Phlotonics Inc., a biosensing company founded in 2022 and headquartered in Rochester, NY, is developing advanced photonic sensor platforms for real-time, label-free biomolecular analysis. The company's genesis stems from collaborative research at the University of Rochester, with strategic partnerships involving institutions like RIT, SUNY Poly, and AIM Photonics. The founding team consists of CEO Michael “Mickey” Bryan, PhD, and CTO Daniel Steiner, PhD, who co-developed the core technology, alongside Scientific Advisor Benjamin Miller, PhD. Dr. Bryan provides executive management experience, while Dr. Steiner has a background in laboratory-to-consumer commercial production. Dr. Miller is noted as a serial entrepreneur and a principal investigator on key grants that supported the technology's development. The firm's primary focus is on the drug discovery, regenerative medicine, and biomanufacturing markets. Phlotonics is engineering light-powered chips and integrating its photonic sensors into microphysiological systems (MPS), also known as 'organs-on-a-chip'. This allows researchers and contract research organizations to observe how human cells respond to drugs in real-time, offering a more precise and efficient alternative to conventional methods like animal testing. This approach aims to provide more reliable data earlier in the drug development process, potentially reducing the high failure rates in Phase I clinical trials. A significant application of their technology is the BioproSense™ platform, designed for real-time bioprocess monitoring. In a notable project, Phlotonics was awarded a $1.1 million contract from AIM Photonics to adapt this platform for regenerative biomanufacturing. This 18-month effort is centered on creating continuous, non-invasive monitoring for the large-scale production of insulin-producing cells from stem cells, a crucial step for developing new diabetes treatments. Additionally, the company secured a $1.45 million subcontract to develop biosensing tools for lung and brain tissue models, enabling the study of how respiratory viruses can cause neurological symptoms. Initial revenue is expected from the deployment of these biosensing systems and consumables to researchers for feedback. Keywords: photonic sensors, biosensing, point-of-care diagnostics, medical diagnostic technology, biomanufacturing, drug discovery, real-time monitoring, label-free analysis, microphysiological systems, organs-on-a-chip, regenerative medicine, cell therapy, biomolecular analysis, tissue models, contract research organizations, clinical trials, vaccine efficacy, immune response, Michael Bryan, Daniel Steiner

column5: Real-time image resizing as a service powering @Kickstarter, @guardian, @eventbrite, and many more.
column6: imgix operates as a platform for processing and delivering visual media, enabling clients to manage, optimize, and distribute images and videos in real-time. The company was founded in 2011 by Chris Zacharias and is headquartered in San Francisco. It emerged from the Y Combinator accelerator program in the summer of 2011.The inspiration for imgix stemmed from founder Chris Zacharias's experience as the second full-time web developer at YouTube. While there, he identified that oversized video thumbnails were negatively impacting user experience and began seeking optimization methods. After leaving YouTube in 2010, he started imgix from his living room, initially creating a website for the band The Lonely Island. Zacharias holds a degree in New Media from the Rochester Institute of Technology and previously worked as a software engineer at Xerox before joining YouTube.The company's business model is centered on providing a comprehensive solution for visual media that avoids the need for clients to build complex in-house imaging pipelines. It serves a wide range of customers, from individual developers and marketers to large enterprises like Google, Netflix, and Spotify. Revenue is generated through a credit-based subscription model where users pay for capacity across media management, delivery, and transformation features. This structure allows all customers access to the full suite of tools, including advanced AI and video capabilities.imgix's core product is an end-to-end visual media platform accessed via APIs, SDKs, and integrations. It provides real-time image and video processing, allowing on-the-fly transformations such as resizing, cropping, and stylization through simple URL parameters. The service integrates with a global Content Delivery Network (CDN) to ensure fast delivery worldwide. Key features include an Asset Manager for organizing files, automatic conversion to modern formats like WebP, and intelligent optimizations for performance and visual quality. Recently, the company has incorporated AI-powered tools for tasks like background removal, generative fill, and automatic alt-text creation. The platform is designed to connect directly to a business's existing cloud storage, simplifying integration.Keywords: image processing API, video delivery, content delivery network, CDN, visual media optimization, real-time image transformation, asset management, Y Combinator alumnus, Chris Zacharias, image rendering, video streaming, responsive images, AI image editing, media management, SaaS, cloud-based image service, website performance, digital asset management, media optimization, developer tools, API-first

column5: The first AI driven software test automation system
column6: Appvance.ai provides a software quality assurance platform that leverages generative artificial intelligence to automate testing, aiming to disrupt the software quality assurance market. The company was established in 2012 by a team of AI and test automation experts, with Frank Cohen listed as a founder and Kevin Surace as a co-founder. Surace, a technology innovator with 94 worldwide patents, serves as the Chairman and CTO, while Andre Liao, an executive with nearly three decades of experience in the high-tech sector, holds the position of CEO. Liao's background includes leadership roles and expertise in enterprise technology sales and product development, complemented by a Master's degree from the USC Marshall School of Business and a Bachelor's from the University of California, Santa Cruz. Surace's entrepreneurial journey includes pioneering work on an early smartphone, a human-like AI virtual assistant that influenced GM's OnStar, and receiving accolades such as Inc. Magazine's 2009 Entrepreneur of the Year.  The company's core offering is Appvance IQ (AIQ), a unified test automation platform launched in late 2017. AIQ is designed for large enterprises to enhance the efficiency of their testing teams and the overall quality of their applications. The platform operates on a subscription-based model, with pricing tailored to organizational needs. It utilizes an AI/ML engine to design, generate, and execute tests for complex web and mobile applications, often without human intervention. The system supports a wide range of applications, including web, mobile, SOA services, and Oracle forms. Key features include AI-driven test generation, codeless script creation, unified testing capabilities (functional, performance, security), and integrations with CI/CD tools like Jenkins and Jira. AIQ can automatically generate thousands of test scripts, identify bugs, and provides detailed reporting and analytics. Recently, Appvance launched GENI, a generative AI feature that converts English test cases into test scripts, aiming to make manual testing obsolete.  Financially, Appvance.ai has secured a total of $24.2 million over five funding rounds. A significant milestone was the $13 million Series C funding round in January 2022, led by Arrowroot Capital. This funding was aimed at accelerating global expansion and product development following a period of substantial business growth. The company is headquartered in Santa Clara, California, with additional offices in Costa Rica and India to support its global customer base. column5: Continual Care Solutions Integrating social determinant and clinical care for at-risk and vulnerable populations
column6: Continual Care Solutions, founded in 2018, is a software-as-a-service (SaaS) firm targeting the nonprofit health and human services sector. The company originated when seasoned healthcare leader Sean Ossont, former strategy officer at the University of Rochester Medical Center, partnered with veteran engineer and entrepreneur Mike Kamish. Kamish previously co-founded and served as CEO of Bio-optronics, a successful venture he exited prior to this new undertaking. They assembled a founding team with deep industry experience, including a former CEO of Volunteers of America of Upstate New York and a past vice president for Medicare and Medicaid services at large managed care organizations, to address systemic inefficiencies in social care. The company's core offering is imPowr, an enterprise software platform designed to consolidate disparate data sources into a single, integrated system for its clients. This platform provides tools for nonprofit organizations to streamline processes, manage client well-being through a comprehensive electronic social record, and improve data collection. The goal is to help these organizations operate more efficiently, demonstrate their impact to secure funding, and mitigate risks, allowing them to better focus on their core missions. The business model is primarily subscription-based, with the company serving nonprofit agencies directly. A secondary, more lucrative phase of their strategy involves partnering with healthcare payers (insurers) who fund networks of nonprofits to use the imPowr platform. This enables the payers to aggregate data, allocate resources more effectively, and ultimately reduce healthcare costs driven by social factors. A key milestone includes a partnership with VirtualHealth in March 2021, integrating imPowr with the HELIOS® medical management platform. This collaboration facilitates bi-directional data exchange between community-based social services and clinical healthcare providers, creating a more holistic view of a patient's circumstances. This interoperability is central to the company's value proposition, aiming to bridge the gap between social and clinical models of care to improve outcomes for vulnerable populations. The imPowr platform serves as a Community Information Exchange, enabling better coordination among providers within social care networks. column5: Commercial real estate asset and portfolio management software
column6: Founded in 1985 in Houston, Texas, ARGUS Software carved out a niche by providing essential software solutions for the commercial real estate industry. Their products became indispensable tools for valuation, asset management, and forecasting, establishing themselves as an industry standard taught in numerous universities. The company's offerings enabled real estate professionals, including owners, managers, and financial institutions, to analyze and manage the value of their property investments. The story of ARGUS took a significant turn in 2006 when it was known as Realm Business Solutions Inc. Private equity firm Lightyear Capital, along with Insight Venture Partners, invested in the company, recognizing its potential, and renamed it ARGUS Software, Inc. This new chapter was marked by strategic growth, including two acquisitions that expanded its product line and global reach. The company focused on developing its next-generation platform, ARGUS Enterprise, to manage large-scale real estate portfolios. A major milestone occurred in 2011 when Altus Group acquired ARGUS Software for $130 million. This acquisition integrated ARGUS's leading software with Altus Group's extensive data and advisory services, aiming to create a comprehensive platform for commercial real estate data and analytics. The move was designed to enhance value for clients by combining top-tier software with in-depth market insights, solidifying ARGUS's position in the global real estate technology landscape.

column5: Sharklet Technologies, Inc.
column6: Sharklet Technologies, founded in 2007, operates at the intersection of biotechnology and materials science, commercializing a surface topography technology that inhibits bacterial growth without the use of chemicals, antibiotics, or toxic additives. The company’s core intellectual property is a microscopic pattern, called Sharklet, which is comprised of millions of raised features arranged in a distinct diamond pattern that mimics the properties of shark skin. This structure alone creates a surface that is energetically unfavorable for bacteria to attach, colonize, and form biofilms.The genesis of the company traces back to Dr. Anthony Brennan, a professor of Materials Science and Engineering at the University of Florida. His research, initially funded by the U.S. Office of Naval Research to find a non-toxic anti-fouling solution for ship hulls, led him to question why slow-moving sharks remain free of barnacles and algae. Intrigued, he took an impression of a shark's skin and discovered its unique dermal denticle structure. This led to the development of the Sharklet pattern, which in early tests, reduced green algae settlement by 85%. Realizing the broader implications, particularly for healthcare, Sharklet Technologies was formed, licensing the technology from the university.The firm's business model centers on licensing its patented micro-texture and partnering with manufacturers to integrate the technology into a variety of products. The primary target market is the healthcare sector, where the technology can be applied to medical devices to reduce the incidence of healthcare-associated infections. Products developed or in development include urinary catheters, endotracheal tubes, and wound dressings. For example, the technology has been shown to reduce colonization of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa bacteria by around 70% compared to smooth surfaces. Beyond healthcare, the company offers an adhesively-backed film that can be applied to high-touch surfaces in environments like hospitals, schools, and public transportation. Consumer products, such as yoga mats, have also been part of its portfolio. The company was acquired in 2017 by Peaceful Union, a Chinese private equity firm, a move intended to accelerate the development of medical devices and other applications.Keywords: biomimicry, surface topography, bacterial inhibition, antimicrobial surface, non-toxic, medical devices, healthcare-associated infections, biofilm prevention, micro-pattern, shark skin replication, anti-fouling, material science, surface technology, microbial control, catheter technology, wound care, public health, surface sanitation, chemical-free cleaning, infection control

column5: Integrated and model-driven software platform providing mobile driven management solutions for businesses
column6: Siperian, Inc. operated as a key player in the master data management (MDM) infrastructure market before its acquisition by Informatica Corporation. Founded in April 2001 by brothers Ken and Khoi Hoang, the company established its headquarters in Foster City, California. The initial concept for Siperian originated with Ken Hoang in late 2000, who then brought on his brother Khoi as the second employee. Their journey in the data management space began even earlier, with some team members, like Lesley Hanly, starting at a startup named Delos in 2001, which Siperian subsequently acquired in 2003. The firm specialized in providing a holistic, single view of foundational business entities, often referred to as master data, which includes records for customers, products, employees, and suppliers. Siperian's core product, the Siperian Multidomain MDM Hub, was a flexible software platform designed to allow enterprises to consolidate and manage this critical data from various distributed sources. This platform was notable for its multidomain capability, meaning it was not restricted to a single data type like 'customer' or 'product' but could manage multiple data domains simultaneously on the same platform. This approach enabled clients to start with one business problem and expand to others over time, with nearly half of its customers leveraging the platform for additional data domains beyond their initial implementation. The business model centered on licensing this MDM software to large, data-intensive Global 500 companies across various industries, including prominent names in financial services and life sciences such as Bank of America, State Street Bank, Pfizer, and Shire. A key feature of Siperian's offering was its built-in data governance application, the Business Data Director. This web-based tool empowered business users, not just IT staff, to create, manage, and monitor master data directly, while adhering to governance policies and workflows for review and approval. This proactive approach to data quality, correcting information at the point of entry, was a significant differentiator. On January 28, 2010, Informatica announced its acquisition of Siperian for approximately $130 million in cash. The move was a natural extension for Informatica, as Siperian already used Informatica's identity resolution and address cleansing technologies as core components of its MDM Hub through OEM relationships. The acquisition integrated Siperian's multidomain MDM capabilities into Informatica's broader data integration and data quality platform. Keywords: master data management, MDM, data governance, multidomain MDM, data integration, data quality, customer data integration, product information management, Siperian MDM Hub, Informatica acquisition, single source of truth, data consolidation, enterprise data management, data stewardship, reference data, business entity data, Khoi Hoang, Ken Hoang, Siperian Business Data Director, data hub

column5: Video distributor for young women that delivers movies and series to them via text message
column6: Blue Fever operates as a social wellness platform targeting the Gen Z demographic, providing a hybrid solution that merges social support with mental health resources. Founded in 2014 by Greta McAnany and Lauren Tracy, the company aims to address the growing mental health concerns among young people. The founders, both with backgrounds in filmmaking and media, identified a gap in the online world for genuine, supportive communities. Greta McAnany's personal experience of wanting a 'big sister' figure for support during her youth, combined with witnessing the toxicity of modern social media, directly inspired the creation of Blue Fever. Lauren Tracy's experience in film school highlighted the lack of female representation in media, leading her to co-found organizations to support female creators before channeling that passion into Blue Fever. The company has secured a total of $10.5M in funding across five rounds from investors including Bumble, Amazon's Alexa Fund, Techstars, and Serena Williams. A key milestone was the acquisition of Trill Project, a similar platform for teens, in October 2021, which expanded its user base and community-building capabilities. The business operates a mobile application available on iOS and Android that functions as an anonymous, judgment-free community. Its core service centers around an AI-powered companion named 'Blue,' designed to act as an empathetic 'big sister,' providing emotional guidance and support. Users can create expressive 'Pages,' akin to visual journal entries, to share their thoughts and feelings without the pressure of traditional social media's curated perfection. The platform intentionally avoids direct photo uploads to encourage authentic, 'inside-out' expression rather than focusing on physical appearance. Blue Fever's revenue model is rooted in its position as a venture-backed, generating revenue platform, although specific subscription or B2B partnership details are not publicly detailed. The platform differentiates itself by fostering a non-toxic environment through proprietary filters and by allowing only supportive interactions, such as virtual 'hugs' or 'relates'. The emphasis on anonymity and privacy is a cornerstone of its strategy, aiming to build trust with its young user base who can connect with peers and the AI companion to navigate issues like stress, loneliness, and anxiety. The app has supported over 3 million people and has been recognized as Apple's App of the Day. Keywords: social wellness, mental health, Gen Z, AI companion, anonymous community, emotional support, peer support, digital wellness, safe space, self-expression

column6: CareSmartz360 provides a cloud-based, HIPAA-compliant software solution for home care agencies. The company was established in 2016 after identifying a need in the market for a unified software solution to address the operational inefficiencies and reliance on paper-based systems within a home care franchise. The platform was co-founded by Manipal Dhariwal, who serves as CEO, and Manjeet Dhariwal. Manipal Dhariwal has over two decades of experience in the IT sector and supports a portfolio of more than 20 startups globally. Manjeet Dhariwal has over 25 years of experience launching ventures in the cloud and IT services sectors. The leadership team also includes COO Sanjay Aggarwal, who has over 20 years in IT and product life cycle management, and General Manager John Ogden, an entrepreneur with over 25 years as a senior IT executive who joined Caresmartz in 2012. The company's core offering is an all-in-one, enterprise-grade platform designed for large and multi-location home care agencies, franchises, and facility staffing providers. It centralizes key agency operations, including scheduling, billing, client management, and caregiver management. The business model is based on a Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) subscription, and it targets private-duty home care agencies of various sizes, from growing businesses to large enterprises. The software aims to streamline operations, improve efficiency, and ensure compliance for its clients. Key features of the CareSmartz360 platform include AI-powered tools for functions like caregiver matching and retention, automated scheduling, and multi-payer billing that accommodates Medicaid, VA, long-term care insurance, and private pay. The system incorporates Electronic Visit Verification (EVV) integrations for over 45 states to assist with compliance and audit readiness. For financial management, it offers automated clean claim processing and integrates with QuickBooks. A GPS-enabled mobile application for caregivers allows for real-time shift clock-in/out, schedule viewing, and secure communication. The platform also provides tools for client assessments, care planning, franchise network management, and custom reporting. column5: ReadCoor is pioneering panomic spatial sequencing
column6: ReadCoor is a biotechnology startup that has developed a unique platform for gene analysis. The company's technology uses a method called padlock probe rolling circle amplification chemistry, which is a highly sensitive and specific way of identifying and analyzing genes. This method allows for high sensitivity across a broad range of gene expression, including those that are low expressing or highly degraded. It also has the ability to distinguish between similar genes, such as isoforms.The company's technology works in four steps. First, the probe arms must stably hybridize to their target gene. If only one arm hybridizes, the probe is unstable and will be washed off. The probes also contain a gene-specific barcode sequence that is used to generate a unique optical signature of each gene. Second, only when both probe arms are stably hybridized to the target with a perfect match does proximal probe ligation occur. Third, only ligated probes are amplified, which enriches for true target recognition events and suppresses off-target events. Finally, the amplified products in the samples undergo successive rounds of fluorescent probe hybridization, imaging, and removal. An optical signature specific to each gene is generated.ReadCoor's clients are likely to be in the scientific and medical research fields, where gene analysis is crucial. The company operates in the biotechnology market and its business model is likely based on selling its technology and services to these clients. It makes money by providing a unique, highly sensitive and specific method of gene analysis.Keywords: Biotechnology, Gene Analysis, Padlock Probe Rolling Circle Amplification, High Sensitivity, Specificity, Isoforms, Hybridization, Ligation, Amplification, Optical Signature.

column6: CytoTronics is a pioneering startup that operates at the intersection of cell biology and advanced technology. The company has developed a groundbreaking semiconductor platform, known as Pixel™, which integrates cutting-edge technology with live cell biology and data science. This innovative approach allows researchers to gain unique insights into cell functions at a high throughput, meaning they can analyze a large number of cells quickly and efficiently.CytoTronics primarily serves clients in the pharmaceutical and biotechnology industries, as well as academic research institutions. These clients are involved in drug discovery and development, where understanding cell behavior is crucial. The company's technology is particularly valuable for phenotypic screening, a process used to observe the physical and biochemical characteristics of cells, which is essential in identifying potential drug candidates.Operating in the life sciences market, CytoTronics leverages its advanced semiconductor platform to provide multiplexed live cell readouts at single-cell resolution. This means they can measure multiple parameters from individual cells simultaneously, offering a detailed and comprehensive view of cell functions. The platform is designed to scale without compromise, seamlessly integrating with other systems and utilizing cloud services to handle large datasets.CytoTronics' business model is based on providing its technology as a service to its clients. They make money by offering subscriptions or licensing agreements for their platform, as well as through direct sales of their technology. Additionally, they may offer custom solutions and consulting services to help clients integrate and optimize the use of their platform in their research workflows.In summary, CytoTronics is revolutionizing cell biology research with its advanced semiconductor platform, serving pharmaceutical, biotech, and academic clients by providing high-throughput, detailed cell analysis. Their business model focuses on technology licensing, subscriptions, and consulting services.Keywords: semiconductor platform, cell biology, high throughput, phenotypic screening, drug discovery, biotechnology, pharmaceutical, live cell readouts, data science, cloud integration.

column5: Culture Genesis is a digital studio, focused on remixing digital technology for urban multicultural audiences
column6: Culture Genesis Inc. operates as a media-technology company with a clear focus on empowering multicultural content creators and publishers. Founded in 2018 by Cedric J. Rogers and Shaun Newsum, the firm is headquartered in Santa Monica, California, and has established itself as the largest Black-owned digital video network in the United States.  The company's mission is to address the monetization challenges faced by underrepresented creators in the digital landscape. This vision is driven by the founders' extensive backgrounds in technology and media. Cedric J. Rogers, the CEO, is a former Apple executive with nearly a decade of experience in business development and developer relations at the tech giant. His co-founder, Shaun Newsum, brings valuable expertise from his time as a senior software engineer at Major League Baseball and VEVO. Their combined experience provides a strong foundation for a tech-driven media company aimed at serving multicultural audiences. Rogers' journey includes co-founding other startups and participating in the prestigious Y Combinator program, experiences that sharpened his focus on creating a purpose-driven company to support multicultural creators.  Culture Genesis' business model centers on providing creators with the tools and opportunities to increase their revenue. The company operates a network of over 120 creators, including prominent figures like Steve Harvey, Cam Newton, and Kai Cenat. A key revenue stream is derived from selling advertising inventory on YouTube for its own properties and for the creators and publishers within its network. This is often structured as a revenue-sharing agreement, where Culture Genesis typically splits the ad revenue 50/50 with its partners. The company has secured significant advertising deals with major brands such as Coca-Cola, Ford, and Unilever. A pivotal moment in its history was the acquisition of the All Def brand in 2019, which significantly raised Culture Genesis' visibility and provided a substantial library of content.  To further support its creator network, Culture Genesis offers content licensing opportunities, providing upfront capital for content production. This is exemplified by a $25 million partnership with Jellysmack, aimed at providing financing to underrepresented creators on YouTube to help close the pay gap they often face. The company also provides a dashboard for creators to track advertising revenue from all their social media accounts in one place and facilitates direct payouts. Recently, Culture Genesis has expanded its capabilities by partnering with VTAGZ By Mammoth, a SaaS platform that enables brands to track the impact of creator-driven campaigns on in-store purchases through SMS-based cashback campaigns, offering a full-funnel marketing solution.  Keywords: multicultural creators, digital media, creator economy, ad revenue, monetization, Black-owned media, YouTube network, content licensing, influencer marketing, digital video, brand partnerships, All Def Digital, Cedric J. Rogers, Shaun Newsum, creator financing, media-tech, Gen Z marketing, diverse audiences, retail attribution, social media monetization

column5: Record and share your dining experiences
column6: Fondu, founded in 2010 by Gauri Manglik, Mike Lewis, and Orion Burt, operated as a social network centered on concise food and drink reviews. The New York City-based company was initially launched under the name SpotOn in May 2011 at Tech Crunch Disrupt, with a focus on a restaurant recommendation engine using Foursquare check-in data. The founders observed that users were more engaged with rating places and sharing these with friends rather than the recommendation feature itself. This insight led to a pivot, and the company rebranded as Fondu, launching its iOS app in November 2011. The Fondu app provided a platform for users to post "bite-sized" reviews of up to 175 characters. This model aimed to lower the barrier to entry compared to more comprehensive review sites like Yelp, fostering a community where users followed friends and trusted food bloggers for recommendations. By focusing on a user's social circle, the platform intended to deliver more relevant and positive reviews. The app utilized Foursquare's database, allowing for reviews of places worldwide. Following its launch, Fondu secured a seed round of $575,000 on November 22, 2011, with Eniac Ventures as the lead investor, joined by the NYU Innovation Fund, Harbor Road Ventures, Blazer Ventures, and several angel investors. The company's trajectory culminated in an "acqhire" by Airbnb on October 3, 2012. As part of the acquisition, the Fondu team joined Airbnb's product group in San Francisco, and the Fondu service was shut down on November 1, 2012. The deal was a strategic move by Airbnb to integrate the team's expertise in social discovery and recommendations to enhance the experience for its own growing community of hosts and guests. While the financial terms were not disclosed, Fondu's investors supported the acquisition, viewing Airbnb's culture and vision as a strong fit. column5: Offering artificial intelligence  that analyzes videos with human-like perception
column6: Resonance AI provides an artificial intelligence-driven platform designed to analyze video content for the media and advertising industries. Founded in 2014 by CEO Tom Chiarella and President Randa Minkarah, the Seattle-based company was formerly known as OneAccord Digital and Transform. Chiarella brought experience from executive roles at General Electric and as a co-founder of Questra, an early Internet of Things company. Minkarah contributed nearly two decades of experience in media and advertising, including a role as SVP of Revenue and Business Development for Fisher Communications. Her realization that the media industry lacked data-based decision-making inspired the company's creation. The company's core business revolves around a SaaS platform that offers clients actionable insights into how their video content is perceived by audiences. It serves a range of clients, including television networks, production studios, broadcasters, and advertisers, with customers like The Weather Channel and News Press Gazette. The business model is subscription-based, with customized pricing depending on usage and required features. By analyzing the creative elements of video, the platform helps content creators understand viewer behavior to increase viewership, subscriptions, and purchases. This service addresses the costly and time-consuming nature of traditional feedback methods like test screenings and surveys. Resonance AI's platform uses machine learning to dissect video content, going beyond simple object tagging to analyze nuanced elements. Its features include the analysis of dialogue, music, pacing, on-screen characters, lighting, and color palettes. The technology makes connections between these components and viewer engagement data, presenting the analysis through intuitive visualizations. This allows clients to identify their most valuable talent, understand which storylines are most effective, and enrich metadata for recommendation engines. In September 2020, the company raised $2.28 million in a Series B round, bringing its total funding to approximately $5.78 million, and was also recognized as a Top Pick at TechCrunch Disrupt in the same month. Keywords: video analytics, audience engagement, media technology, AI content analysis, content intelligence, advertising technology, viewer behavior, machine learning, content performance, creative analysis, broadcast technology, media insights, streaming analytics, entertainment technology, video data, character analysis, narrative analysis, emotional resonance, ad effectiveness

column5: FUSAR - Universal Smart Helmet Technology
column6: FUSAR Technologies was a New Jersey-based startup focused on the connected mobility and wearable technology space, specifically for action sports. Founded in 2013 by childhood friends and enthusiasts Ryan Shearman (CEO), Clayton Patton (CFO), and Todd Rushing (Chief Designer), the company was born from Shearman's personal experience of being in a motorcycle accident. This event catalyzed the mission to create a unified technology platform to enhance safety and connectivity for riders. The firm began its journey in the TechLaunch accelerator program in New Jersey and secured early-stage funding from investors like the Jumpstart New Jersey Angel Network. The company's business was centered on developing and selling a hardware and software ecosystem designed to turn any helmet into a smart helmet. Its target clients were participants in action sports such as motorcycling, cycling, and snowboarding. Revenue was generated through the direct-to-consumer sale of its hardware products, which were initially promoted through a successful Indiegogo crowdfunding campaign that hit its $100,000 goal on the first day. The business model also anticipated potential recurring revenue through software subscriptions. FUSAR's flagship product was the Mohawk, a modular, helmet-mounted device. This single unit integrated an action camera, GPS navigation, a communication system, an activity tracker, and a black box with an emergency response system. Key features included HD video and photo capture, with a "HotShot" function to save the last 15 seconds of footage even when not actively recording. A critical safety component was the automatic crash detection, which used built-in sensors like an accelerometer and gyroscope. Upon detecting a crash, the system would automatically alert pre-set emergency contacts via SMS and email with the user's precise GPS location and lock down the last two minutes of audio, video, and telemetry data as a black box record. The system also enabled group communication for up to 12 users and synced with a companion smartphone app for iOS and Android. After raising a total of $2.32M over several funding rounds, the company ultimately ceased operations around November 2018. Founder Ryan Shearman later went on to co-found Aether Diamonds and Loa Carbon, companies focused on creating carbon-negative diamonds and decarbonization solutions. Keywords: smart helmet, motorcycle technology, action camera, wearable technology, crash detection, emergency alert system, GPS tracking, vehicle black box, rider safety, powersports tech, helmet communication, modular helmet system, Indiegogo hardware, Ryan Shearman, connected mobility, IoT device, action sports electronics, Bluetooth helmet, rider communication, adventure tech, motorcycle safety, wearable camera, smart camera, telemetry data, FUSAR Mohawk

column5: A distributed network of sustainable indoor vertical farms
column6: Nimble Farms, formerly known as Ellicottville Greens, operates a distributed network of indoor vertical farms, addressing the demand for local produce in the Western and Upstate New York regions. The company was founded in 2018 by Gabe Bialkowski and Sal LaTorre. LaTorre, with a background as a chef, transitioned into farming to engage with food on a more profound level. The business model is centered around growing produce within refurbished shipping containers, a modular approach that allows for scalability and rapid deployment. This method also supports enhanced traceability and customization of the growing environment to alter product flavor.The company's core business involves the cultivation of microgreens, leafy greens, herbs, and mushrooms within these controlled environments. These climate-controlled units are outfitted with energy-efficient LED lighting, recirculating water and nutrient systems, and precise environmental controls to enable year-round production of pesticide-free, non-GMO crops. Products, which include varieties of lettuce like Bibb and Butterhead, and herbs such as Genovese and Thai basil, are sold directly to consumers via online orders, as well as to restaurants, institutions, food distributors, and grocery stores. A significant portion of its business comes from direct-to-consumer sales, offering customizable ordering and remote farm subscriptions.In early 2025, the company rebranded to Nimble Farms to better reflect its mission of making fresh, local food accessible to every community. This rebranding coincided with a major retail expansion through a partnership with Tops Friendly Markets, making its Butterhead Living Lettuce available in 152 stores across New York, Pennsylvania, and Vermont. The company has demonstrated consistent growth, raising seed funding and a subsequent Series A round to facilitate expansion, including the acquisition of Vertical Fresh Farms. The long-term strategy involves establishing a national network of indoor farms, either company-owned or operated through partnerships, all managed from its headquarters in Ellicottville, NY.Keywords: indoor vertical farming, hydroponics, controlled environment agriculture, sustainable farming, local produce, leafy greens, herbs, direct-to-consumer, food distribution, agritech, container farming, urban agriculture, food insecurity solutions, Nimble Farms, Gabe Bialkowski, Sal LaTorre, Western New York food market, fresh produce delivery, subscription farming, retail food partnership

column5: Platform that captures and analyzes data to improve the agribusiness process
column6: EIWA operates as an agricultural technology firm focused on data solutions, originally founded in Argentina in 2014 and now headquartered in St. Louis, Missouri. The company was established by Nicolas Otamendi, who serves as CEO, and Marcelo Gowland. Their team is comprised of an interdisciplinary group of agronomy engineers, developers, and precision agriculture specialists. Positioned as a data cloud ecosystem, EIWA secured an undisclosed amount in a Series A funding round in March 2023, led by Serra Ventures, to expand its commercial teams in the US, Brazil, and Argentina and to further develop its platform. Previously, the company had raised \$1.62 million over three rounds from investors including The Yield Lab and Cultivation Capital.  The core of EIWA's business is the Eiwa Vault, a cloud-based platform that provides critical infrastructure for integrating and analyzing agricultural data from disparate sources and formats. The platform is designed to serve a business-to-business market, targeting entities in the farm input sector such as seed and crop protection companies, crop nutrition and biologicals suppliers, and research institutions. Its primary function is to help these clients digitize and standardize the data collection and analysis process for field trials, thereby accelerating their research, development, and go-to-market strategies. The business model is structured as a Software-as-a-Service (SaaS), where clients pay an annual license fee. Pricing is determined by the number of plots or trials and the level of functionality accessed.  The Eiwa Vault platform enables clients to manage and interpret complex information by turning various data layers, including those from drones and IoT devices, into structured, actionable insights. It helps users geolocate trial plots, automate data collection through a scouting app, and analyze field trial data at scale to improve yields and margins. For instance, it assists in standardizing seed trial data management, tracking environmental impacts on seed development, and monitoring fields to protect crops from changing conditions. By offering a centralized platform, EIWA allows for more consistent data analysis across multiple locations, ultimately helping clients mobilize their agronomic knowledge and make more informed decisions.
