column5: Platypus TV operates an online platform that turns conversations into threads around specific episodes of television shows
column6: Platypus TV operates as a social web platform designed to enhance the television viewing experience by allowing users to engage in time-stamped conversations related to specific episodes. This core function enables viewers to share their thoughts and reactions as they watch their favorite shows, regardless of when they choose to view the content. The platform was co-founded by Nomi Foster and Connor, whose last name is not publicly available. Connor's background is in game design and development, holding a Bachelor of Science from the Rochester Institute of Technology with a minor in web development, which provides the technical expertise for building the platform. The business serves television viewers who want a more interactive and social experience with their content. By time-stamping comments, Platypus TV creates a virtual shared viewing experience that is not dependent on watching live. This extends the life of a show's social buzz beyond its initial air date. The company's revenue model appears to be centered around advertising, offering brands an opportunity to connect with a highly engaged audience throughout the extended life cycle of a television episode. The platform's main feature is the ability to attach comments to specific moments in a video, creating a running commentary that other users can read and contribute to at the same point in the episode. This facilitates a continuous conversation around the show, fostering a community of fans. The benefit for users is the ability to participate in a social viewing experience on their own schedule, avoiding spoilers while still feeling connected to other fans. The unique selling point is this asynchronous social layer on top of on-demand television content, which differentiates it from live-tweeting or general social media discussions that are not tied to the video timeline. Keywords: social TV, second screen, time-stamped comments, interactive television, asynchronous viewing, fan engagement, episode discussion, TV community, social viewing platform, on-demand social experience, television advertising, media and entertainment, Nomi Foster, Connor, Syracuse startup

column6: In February 2017, in Copenhagen, Denmark, a new venture called Lokalist ApS was founded by Tegan Spinner and Ralf Patrik Blaga. Their idea was to build a different kind of digital marketplace. Instead of the usual haggling between buyers and sellers, they envisioned a platform for used electronics that was smarter and more efficient. The core of their playbook was to use technology to solve a human problem. They focused on behavioral economics and automated price negotiation to create a better experience. The mission was to help both the buyer and the seller get the best possible price instantly, making the process simple and safe. This approach aimed to encourage the up-cycling of products, contributing to a circular economy. With Tegan Spinner as CEO and Ralf Patrik Blaga as CTO, the company set out to build its platform. They attracted initial funding from a group of individual investors who believed in their vision for a more efficient second-hand market. While not a headline-grabbing IPO or a major acquisition, this early support was crucial. Lokalist ApS's story is one of targeted innovation, aiming to carve out a niche by fundamentally improving the transaction process for used goods.

column6: Founded by Lauren and Leah, two dance educators passionate about simplifying the business of dance, Studiotogo (studiotogo.com) operates primarily as a resource for dance studio owners and teachers. The founders' journey began with a desire to help fellow female entrepreneurs in the dance world streamline their operations, boost revenue, and achieve a better work-life balance. The company's core business revolves around a B2B model, providing a comprehensive suite of digital products and services tailored for dance education. This includes preschool dance curriculums, detailed lesson plans, and choreography delivered through their 'Studio To Go Dance' platform. A key offering is the "Little Swan Dance" subscription, which transitions into a monthly licensing agreement for continued content access after the first year. Beyond curriculum, Studiotogo extends its support through business coaching programs like "One-on-One" and "Beyond the Studio," designed to guide studio owners in transforming their businesses and daily lives. Expanding its market reach, the company also operates a direct-to-consumer, on-demand streaming service. This platform offers a variety of ballet and other dance activities, ranging from short two-minute clips to full 40-minute lessons, all featuring age-appropriate content taught by professional instructors. This service functions on a subscription basis, priced at $9.99 per month following a 30-day free trial, making dance accessible for families and individuals to enjoy at home. The terms explicitly restrict for-profit dance professionals from using the family subscription for teaching purposes, segmenting their B2B and B2C client bases. column6: Sprig is a product experience insights platform designed for companies to conduct user research throughout the product development lifecycle. The firm was founded in 2018 by Ryan Glasgow, who now serves as the CEO. His background as a product manager at several startups that saw successful exits, including Weebly (acquired by Square) and Vurb (acquired by Snapchat), informed the company's direction. Glasgow's experience watching user researchers manually sift through data highlighted a need for a more scalable solution, leading to the creation of Sprig. Originally named UserLeap, the company's mission is to help teams understand user needs and make customer-informed product decisions. The company operates on a software-as-a-service (SaaS) business model, serving product managers, user researchers, and designers at high-growth technology companies. Clients include prominent names like Notion, Dropbox, Loom, Square, and Adobe. Revenue is generated through subscriptions to its platform, which enables businesses to gather and analyze customer insights in real-time. Sprig has secured significant venture capital funding, raising a total of $88 million across several rounds from investors including Andreessen Horowitz, Accel, First Round Capital, and Figma Ventures. The most recent funding round in August 2022 brought in $30 million, intended to accelerate product development and expand service lines. Sprig's platform offers a suite of tools to capture user feedback directly within a client's product. Key features include In-Product Surveys, Concept & Usability Testing, Session Replays, and Heatmaps. These tools allow businesses to test ideas, prototypes, and designs before development, and to continuously monitor and optimize live features. A core component of the platform is its use of artificial intelligence to analyze open-text responses and behavioral data, automatically identifying themes, patterns, and areas of user friction. This AI-powered analysis turns qualitative feedback into actionable insights, helping teams understand the "why" behind user actions and make data-backed decisions more efficiently. The platform integrates with other product tools, allowing for seamless incorporation of insights into the development workflow. Keywords: product experience insights, user research platform, customer feedback, in-product surveys, usability testing, concept testing, AI-powered analysis, product development lifecycle, user behavior analytics, real-time insights, session replay, heatmaps, product management software, SaaS, user segmentation, customer sentiment, product optimization, Ryan Glasgow, Andreessen Horowitz, Accel

column5: Internet technology company providing a platform enabling users tag people, places, and objects in an image
column6: Stipple, founded in August 2010 by former record company executive Rey Flemings and co-founder Michael Dungan, was a San Francisco-based company that developed an image-based advertising technology platform. The company's primary offering was a platform that allowed for the tagging of people, places, and products directly within images, effectively turning static pictures into interactive storefronts. This was born from the realization that when photos are shared across the web, the original context and data, such as captions and tags, are often lost. Stipple's technology ensured this information remained with the image, creating a persistent connection. The business model centered on monetizing web imagery. Publishers could install a simple line of JavaScript on their websites, which would enable Stipple's smart tags on all their photos. When a user's cursor hovered over a tagged item, a dot would appear, revealing information like product details, pricing, and a direct link to purchase. This system, dubbed "AdWords For Images" by TechCrunch, created a new revenue stream for publishers, who would get paid for audience engagement and click-throughs, while brands gained a novel way to market their products directly from editorial content. Clients included major brands such as 20th Century Fox, Zappos, and Saks Fifth Avenue. The platform also offered analytics for brands to track campaign distribution and image viewability. Stipple launched its first public offering in August 2010 and quickly secured a $2 million seed funding round in November 2010, co-led by Kleiner Perkins Caufield & Byers and Floodgate. The investor list was notable, including figures like Justin Timberlake, Naval Ravikant, and Matt Mullenweg. Over its lifetime, the company raised a total of $10 million across three funding rounds, with its final Series A round in December 2012. The company launched several products, including Stipple Lens for photo agencies and Stipple Pipeline for brands to tag their products. It also introduced Stipple Marketplace, which provided publishers with a searchable database of over a million images pre-loaded with monetizable tags. Despite its early traction and significant backing, Stipple was eventually acquired by an undisclosed firm in a private transaction and the product was ultimately shuttered. column5: A photo-sharing service that allows users to collect, group, and share photos and albums
column6: PhotoRocket, founded in 2009 by serial entrepreneur Scott Lipsky, was a Seattle-based startup designed to streamline the process of photo sharing. The company entered the market as digital photography was increasingly shifting to mobile phones, aiming to provide a hassle-free method for users to organize and distribute their pictures. Lipsky's extensive background in technology, including key roles at GameStop, Barnes & Noble, Amazon, and as a co-founder of the digital marketing firm aQuantive (sold to Microsoft for $6 billion), informed the company's direction. He identified a need for a simpler sharing solution as the volume of digital photos began to expand rapidly.The business operated as a multiplatform tool with versions for Windows, iOS, and Android, enabling users to share photos from their devices to various social networks with a single action. The service worked by allowing a user to right-click on a photo or group of photos, select contacts, and choose the destination social sites, such as Facebook and Shutterfly. PhotoRocket's model was focused on user convenience, positioning itself as a central hub for photo distribution. The company also planned to incorporate the sale of photo products like books, calendars, and greeting cards. Its target clients were everyday consumers looking to manage and share their growing digital photo collections across an increasingly fragmented landscape of social media platforms.The company launched an alpha version for Windows in late 2010 and had versions for Mac and iPhone in development. PhotoRocket secured approximately $2.35 million in funding over two rounds, with notable investment from Denny Hill Capital. Despite its initial funding and the clear need it aimed to address, the company ceased operations, and its website became inactive as of February 2016. column5: Submit your customer service issue with GripeO
column6: GripeO, founded in 2012, operates as a social consumer complaint and review platform. The Buffalo, New York-based company was established by a team including Mike Klanac, Nicholas Campanile, Stephen Makula, Richard Panek, Jim Proulx, and Mark Taylor. Initially launched as a complaint management system, the platform provided a consolidated service for users to submit feedback on businesses through its website and mobile application. The original concept allowed consumers to post grievances, which GripeO would then direct to the company involved. The service included tools to amplify the visibility of these complaints via social media. For businesses, GripeO offered free tools for mediation and planned to generate revenue through premium subscriptions, selling ignored complaints as leads in a 'Complaint Marketplace', and targeted advertising based on user complaint patterns. The company secured $220,000 in seed funding, including a $200,000 round in 2015, to support a national rollout. The platform's stated goal was to enhance transparency between consumers and businesses, positioning itself as a modern alternative to the Better Business Bureau. However, the original business appears to have ceased operations around 2016. The domain was later acquired by a new, unknown owner around 2020. Since this change, the site has faced allegations of publishing unverified and negative content, with some users and businesses claiming the platform is used for extortion through paid removal services. The current iteration of the site presents itself as a platform for unbiased reviews and investigations, hosting consumer news and expert opinions to inform the public. column5: Impact. Results. Interactive Solutions Since 1991
column6: Founded in 1999 by brothers Joel and Dan Boblit, BigBadToyStore, Inc. operates as a prominent online retailer in the collectible toys market. The company's genesis stems from Joel Boblit's personal passion for collecting 80s action figures, which he turned into a full-time venture after graduating from college. Initially, the business focused on buying and reselling vintage toys on platforms like eBay before transitioning to new retail items. The business operates through its e-commerce platform, bigbadtoystore.com, serving a global client base of collectors. Its revenue model is direct-to-consumer retail, offering a vast inventory of action figures, statues, and model kits from a wide array of brands including Hasbro, Mattel, and Bandai, among others. The company sources products from major manufacturers and features items from popular franchises such as Transformers, Marvel, DC Comics, Star Wars, and various anime series. A key feature of the company's service is the "Pile of Loot" system. This allows customers to consolidate multiple orders over a period of up to 90 days into a single shipment, providing a cost-effective shipping solution. The business started as a self-funded enterprise, initially run out of the founders' home with the help of their parents, and has since expanded its infrastructure significantly, now operating from a 52,000 square foot warehouse in Somerset, Wisconsin. Joel Boblit manages vendor relationships, finance, and long-term strategy, while Dan Boblit is responsible for operations and website development. The company positions itself as a reliable source for collectors, with a focus on careful packaging and customer service to cater to clients who value the condition of the items and their packaging. column5: Gamified business coaching and education platform
column6: Youtego LLC presents business education through a gamified lens, suggesting that succeeding in business is akin to winning a video game. The company's philosophy centers on optimizing profit over revenue by manipulating key business variables. It provides tools and frameworks, such as advertising calculators and business model math sheets, to help entrepreneurs understand and improve their financial performance. The platform's approach involves breaking down business into different levels, from beginner to advanced, similar to video game progression. Beginners focus on creating a minimum viable product, while advanced users work on scaling with robust systems and team building. The curriculum emphasizes the importance of decreasing customer acquisition costs and service delivery expenses while improving product quality and customer satisfaction to achieve higher profitability. Youtego also highlights concepts like 'skill trees' for developing business competencies, 'boss battles' representing business challenges, and creating a 'quest log' to define actionable next steps for large projects. column5: Online food delivery app
column6: Yumist was a food-tech company founded in November 2014 by Alok Jain, former CMO of Zomato, and Abhimanyu Maheshwari, an experienced restaurateur. The venture was born from Jain's passion for food and technology and Maheshwari's background in the hospitality industry, including his work rebranding and scaling restaurant chains. Yumist aimed to address the demand for convenient, home-style meals for busy urban professionals in India, a market then dominated by unorganized players like dabbawalas and corporate canteens. The company operated on a full-stack, cloud-kitchen model, preparing meals in-house to maintain control over quality, consistency, and cost. This model allowed Yumist to offer daily changing menus of comfort food at affordable price points, starting from INR 100. Customers could order through a mobile app, and the company leveraged technology, including a demand prediction algorithm, to manage its supply chain and ensure speedy delivery, at times achieving a 15-minute delivery window. The business primarily served office-goers and residents in Gurgaon, South Delhi, and Noida from its central and mega-kitchens. Yumist secured a total of $3.37 million in funding over three rounds from investors including Unilazer Ventures and Orios Venture Partners. The company showed promising metrics, such as acquiring customers for Rs 180 and recovering that cost within 45 days, with 70% of monthly orders coming from repeat customers. Delivery outlets were reportedly breaking even at just 70 orders per day. However, the company faced significant challenges. A premature expansion to Bengaluru in early 2016 without establishing a local kitchen led to operational issues and a subsequent shutdown of services in that city in May 2016. Concurrently, the launch of a large 12,000 sq ft mega-kitchen in the Delhi-NCR region increased expenses at a critical time. Ultimately, Yumist struggled to raise further capital amid a shifting investor sentiment towards the high-burn food-tech sector and ceased operations in September 2017.
